subject: ap-chemistry
title: Halogen Chemistry: IMF, Electrochemistry & Equilibrium
stimulus: Answer the following questions relating to the chemistry of the halogens.

parts.prompt:
The molecular formulas of diatomic bromine, chlorine, fluorine, and iodine are written below. Circle the
formula of the molecule that has the longest bond length. Justify your choice in terms of atomic structure.
Br Cl F $I_2$ 2 2 2
A chemistry teacher wants to prepare Br
. The teacher has access to the following three reagents: NaBr(aq),
Cl
(g), and $I_2$(s).
Half-Reaction E° at 25°C (V)
Br + 2 e − → 2 Br − 1.07
Cl + 2 e − → 2 Cl − 1.36
I + 2 e − → 2 I − 0.53
parts.scoring_notes: I$_2$ has the longest bond length. Bond length increases with atomic size. Going down Group 17, atoms have more electron shells → larger atomic radius. I is the largest of F, Cl, Br, I. The I−I bond length is greater than Br−Br > Cl−Cl > F−F because each I atom has more electron shells (5 principal shells) than Cl (3), Br (4), or F (2), resulting in a longer internuclear distance.

scoring_points.description: For identifying I$_2$ as having the longest bond length with atomic structure justification: 1 point
scoring_points.official_rubric: I$_2$ has the longest bond length because the radius of the I atom is greater than the radii of the other halogen atoms. Thus, the distance between the nuclei of atoms in I$_2$ is greater than it is in smaller halogens. 1 point is earned for circling I$_2$ and providing a valid explanation.

sample_responses.response: I$_2$ has the longest bond length. Iodine is in Period 5, giving it 5 electron shells and a much larger atomic radius than F (Period 2), Cl (Period 3), or Br (Period 4). The larger the atom, the greater the internuclear distance in the diatomic molecule.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point: correctly identifies I$_2$ and justifies with atomic size increasing down the group.

sample_responses.response: F$_2$ has the longest bond because fluorine is the most electronegative halogen.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not earn the point: F$_2$ has the SHORTEST bond (smallest atoms). Electronegativity is not the relevant property — atomic radius determines bond length.

required_elements: I$_2$ has the longest bond length.
Iodine is the largest halogen (most electron shells, largest atomic radius) → longest I−I bond distance.
alternatives.correct_example: I$_2$ has the longest bond length. I is in Period 5 (5 electron shells), while Br is in Period 4, Cl is in Period 3, and F is in Period 2. Going down a group, atomic radius increases as additional electron shells are added. Larger atoms have greater internuclear distances when bonded → longer bond.

wrong_examples: Saying F$_2$ has the longest bond — F is the smallest halogen, so F$_2$ has the SHORTEST bond of the group.
Saying Br$_2$ — Br is larger than Cl and F but smaller than I.
common_traps: Bond length increases going DOWN a group (larger atoms = longer bond). Iodine (Period 5) is the largest of the four halogens → I$_2$ has the longest bond.

parts.prompt:
Using the data in the table above, write the balanced equation for the thermodynamically favorable
reaction that will produce Br
when the teacher combines two of the reagents. Justify that the reaction is
thermodynamically favorable by calculating the value of E° for the reaction.
Br
and Cl
can react to form the compound BrCl.
parts.scoring_notes: To produce Br$_2$: need to oxidize Br$^-$ (from NaBr). To reduce Br$^-$ to Br$_2$ requires an oxidizing agent stronger than Br$_2$ itself. Cl$_2$ has E°(Cl$_2$/Cl$^-$) = +1.36 V > E°(Br$_2$/Br$^-$) = +1.07 V, so Cl$_2$ can oxidize Br$^-$. Reaction: Cl$_2$(g) + 2 NaBr(aq) → Br$_2$ + 2 NaCl(aq) OR ionic: Cl$_2$(g) + 2 Br$^-$(aq) → Br$_2$ + 2 Cl$^-$(aq). E° = E°(cathode) − E°(anode) = 1.36 − 1.07 = +0.29 V > 0 → thermodynamically favorable.

scoring_points.description: For the correct balanced reaction producing Br$_2$: 1 point
scoring_points.official_rubric: $2\text{ Br}^-(aq) + \text{Cl}_2(g) \rightarrow \text{Br}_2 + 2\text{ Cl}^-(aq)$. 1 point is earned for the correct balanced equation.

sample_responses.response: $\text{Cl}_2(g) + 2\text{ Br}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{Br}_2(aq) + 2\text{ Cl}^-(aq)$. Cl$_2$ is a stronger oxidizing agent than Br$_2$ (higher E°), so it can oxidize Br$^-$ to Br$_2$.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point: correctly balanced ionic equation with Cl$_2$ as oxidant and Br$^-$ as reductant producing Br$_2$.
Does not earn the point: I$_2$ (E° = 0.53 V) cannot oxidize Br$^-$ because Br$_2$ (E° = 1.07 V) is a stronger oxidizing agent than I$_2$. The reaction would be non-spontaneous.

required_elements: Cl$_2$(g) + 2 NaBr(aq) → Br$_2$ + 2 NaCl(aq) OR ionic form: Cl$_2$(g) + 2 Br$^-$(aq) → Br$_2$ + 2 Cl$^-$(aq)
I$_2$(s) would not work because E°(I$_2$/I$^-$) = 0.53 V < E°(Br$_2$/Br$^-$) = 1.07 V — I$_2$ cannot oxidize Br$^-$.
alternatives.correct_example:
Cl$_2$(g) + 2 Br$^-$(aq) → Br$_2$(aq) + 2 Cl$^-$(aq)
[Cl$_2$ is reduced (cathode); Br$^-$ is oxidized (anode). Cl$_2$ can oxidize Br$^-$ because E°(Cl$_2$/Cl$^-$) > E°(Br$_2$/Br$^-$).]

wrong_examples: Combining I$_2$ + NaBr — I$_2$ cannot oxidize Br$^-$ because I$_2$ is a weaker oxidizing agent than Br$_2$ (lower E°).
Writing the reverse reaction — Br$_2$ would be consumed, not produced.
common_traps: A species with higher reduction potential is a stronger oxidizing agent. Cl$_2$ (E° = 1.36 V) > Br$_2$ (E° = 1.07 V) > I$_2$ (E° = 0.53 V). Cl$_2$ oxidizes Br$^-$; I$_2$ does not.

scoring_points.description: For E° = +0.29 V justifying thermodynamic favorability: 1 point
scoring_points.official_rubric: $E° = E°(\text{reduced species}) - E°(\text{oxidized species}) = 1.36\text{ V} - 1.07\text{ V} = +0.29\text{ V}$. Because E° for the reaction has a positive value, the reaction is thermodynamically favorable. 1 point is earned for the correct calculation of $E°$.

sample_responses.response: $E°_{cell} = E°_{cathode} - E°_{anode} = 1.36\text{ V} - 1.07\text{ V} = +0.29\text{ V}$. Since $E° > 0$, $\Delta G° < 0$ and the reaction is thermodynamically favorable.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point: correct formula, correct values assigned to cathode (Cl$_2$) and anode (Br$^-$), correct positive result.
Does not earn the point: E°$_{cell}$ = E°$_{cathode}$ − E°$_{anode}$, not the sum. Adding gives an incorrect value.

alternatives.correct_example:
$E°_{\text{cell}} = E°_{\text{cathode}} - E°_{\text{anode}} = 1.36\text{ V} - 1.07\text{ V} = +0.29\text{ V}$
Since $E°_{\text{cell}} > 0$, $\Delta G° < 0$ and the reaction is thermodynamically favorable.

wrong_examples: Adding E° values (1.36 + 1.07 = 2.43 V) — E°$_{cell}$ = E°$_{cathode}$ − E°$_{anode}$, not the sum.
Getting a negative E° — if signs are correct, Cl$_2$ is cathode (higher E°) and Br$^-$/Br$_2$ is anode (lower E°).
common_traps: E°$_{cell}$ = E°$_{cathode}$ − E°$_{anode}$. Cathode = reduction = higher reduction potential. Anode = oxidation = lower reduction potential.

parts.prompt:
The boiling point of Br
is 332 K, whereas the boiling point of BrCl is 278 K. Explain this difference in
boiling point in terms of all the intermolecular forces present between molecules of each substance.
The compound BrCl can decompose into Br
and Cl
, as represented by the balanced chemical equation below.
2 BrCl(g)  $Br_2$(g) + Cl (g) ΔH ° = 1.6 kJ/mol
2 2 rxn
A 0.100 mole sample of pure BrCl(g) is placed in a previously evacuated, rigid 2.00 L container at 298 K.
Eventually the system reaches equilibrium according to the equation above.
parts.scoring_notes: Br$_2$ is nonpolar (symmetric, same atoms on each side) → only London dispersion forces (LDF). BrCl is polar (Br and Cl have different electronegativities, δ+ on Br end, δ- on Cl end) → dipole–dipole forces AND London dispersion forces. Despite having more types of IMF, BrCl has a lower boiling point because: Br$_2$ has a larger electron cloud (2 Br atoms, 35 electrons each) → stronger LDFs than BrCl (35 + 17 electrons). The stronger LDFs in Br$_2$ outweigh the dipole–dipole + LDF combination in BrCl.

scoring_points.description: For correctly identifying all IMFs and explaining that stronger LDFs in Br$_2$ dominate: 1 point
scoring_points.official_rubric: The only intermolecular attractions in Br$_2$(l) are London forces, while those in BrCl(l) include both London forces and dipole-dipole forces. However, due to the greater polarizability of the electron cloud of Br$_2$ compared to that of BrCl, the London forces in Br$_2$(l) are stronger than the combined intermolecular forces in BrCl(l). Thus, the boiling point of Br$_2$(l) is greater than that of BrCl(l). 1 point is earned for identifying the intermolecular forces in each substance. 1 point is earned for a valid explanation.

sample_responses.response: Br$_2$ is nonpolar → only London dispersion forces (LDF). BrCl is polar (Br and Cl have different electronegativities) → dipole-dipole forces plus LDF. However, Br$_2$ has a larger, more polarizable electron cloud (2 Br atoms, total 70 electrons) than BrCl (52 electrons), giving it stronger LDF that exceed the total IMF in BrCl. Therefore Br$_2$ has a higher boiling point.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns both sub-points: correctly identifies all IMFs in both molecules and explains that stronger LDF in Br$_2$ dominates.

sample_responses.response: BrCl has dipole-dipole forces plus LDF, so it has stronger overall IMF and should have a higher boiling point than Br$_2$.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not earn the explanation point: ignores that Br$_2$'s LDF are stronger due to greater polarizability. More types of IMF does not automatically mean higher boiling point.

required_elements: Br$_2$: only LDF (nonpolar). BrCl: dipole–dipole AND LDF (polar).
Br$_2$ has a larger, more polarizable electron cloud (70 electrons total) than BrCl (52 electrons total) → stronger LDFs → higher boiling point.
alternatives.correct_example: Br$_2$ is nonpolar (symmetric) → only London dispersion forces. BrCl is polar (Br vs. Cl, different electronegativities) → dipole–dipole forces + London dispersion forces. However, Br$_2$ (70 total electrons) has a larger, more polarizable electron cloud than BrCl (52 electrons), giving Br$_2$ stronger LDF interactions. The stronger LDFs in Br$_2$ overcome the additional dipole–dipole forces in BrCl → Br$_2$ has higher boiling point.

wrong_examples: Saying BrCl should have a higher BP because it has more IMF types — LDF strength (polarizability) can outweigh dipole–dipole when the less polar molecule is much larger.
Ignoring LDFs in Br$_2$ — all molecules have LDFs; cannot list only dipole–dipole for BrCl.
common_traps: More IMF types does NOT always mean higher BP. Must compare IMF strengths. Larger molecules with more electrons have stronger LDFs — can exceed dipole–dipole in smaller polar molecules.

parts.prompt: Calculate the pressure in the container before equilibrium is established.
parts.scoring_notes: Before equilibrium: 0.100 mol BrCl, T = 298 K, V = 2.00 L. Ideal gas law: P = nRT/V = (0.100 mol)(0.08206 L·atm/mol·K)(298 K) / 2.00 L = 2.45/2.00 = 1.22 atm.

scoring_points.description: For the correct initial pressure = 1.22 atm using PV = nRT: 1 point
scoring_points.official_rubric: $P = \frac{nRT}{V} = \frac{(0.100\text{ mol})(0.08206\text{ L atm mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1})(298\text{ K})}{2.00\text{ L}} = 1.22\text{ atm}$. 1 point is earned for a correct pressure with consistent units.

sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point: correct ideal gas law calculation with appropriate units.
Does not earn the point: units are incorrect. Using R = 0.08206 L·atm/mol·K gives P in atm, not kPa.

wrong_examples: Using T in °C (298°C instead of 298 K) — T = 298 K already, not 298°C. If problem had given 25°C, convert to 298 K.
Dividing by volume in mL instead of liters.
common_traps: PV = nRT: rearrange to P = nRT/V. Units: R = 0.08206 L·atm/mol·K, T in K, V in L → P in atm.

parts.prompt:
Write the expression for the equilibrium constant, $K_{eq}$
, for the decomposition of BrCl.
After the system has reached equilibrium, 42 percent of the original BrCl sample has decomposed.
parts.scoring_notes: For 2 BrCl(g) ⇌ Br$_2$(g) + Cl$_2$(g): $K_{eq} = \frac{[\text{Br}_2][\text{Cl}_2]}{[\text{BrCl}]^2}$ (or in terms of partial pressures: $K_p = \frac{P_{\text{Br}_2} \cdot P_{\text{Cl}_2}}{(P_{\text{BrCl}})^2}$). Products in numerator, reactants in denominator, each raised to their stoichiometric coefficient.

scoring_points.description: For a correct equilibrium expression: 1 point
scoring_points.official_rubric: $K_{eq} = \frac{[\text{Br}_2][\text{Cl}_2]}{[\text{BrCl}]^2}$ OR $K_{eq} = \frac{P_{\text{Br}_2}P_{\text{Cl}_2}}{(P_{\text{BrCl}})^2}$. 1 point is earned for a correct equilibrium expression.

sample_responses.response: $K_c = \frac{[\text{Br}_2][\text{Cl}_2]}{[\text{BrCl}]^2}$. Products in numerator, reactant in denominator squared (coefficient of BrCl = 2).
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point: correct products over reactants expression with stoichiometric exponents.
Does not earn the point: this is the equilibrium expression for the reverse reaction. Products (Br$_2$ and Cl$_2$) must be in the numerator.

alternatives.correct_example:
$K_c = \frac{[\text{Br}_2][\text{Cl}_2]}{[\text{BrCl}]^2}$
Products (Br$_2$ and Cl$_2$, each coefficient 1) in numerator; reactant (BrCl, coefficient 2) squared in denominator.

wrong_examples: Not squaring [BrCl] — the coefficient of BrCl is 2, so it must be squared in the denominator.
Putting reactant in the numerator — products in the numerator, reactants in the denominator.
common_traps: $K_{eq}$ = product concentrations raised to stoichiometric coefficients divided by reactant concentrations raised to stoichiometric coefficients. BrCl has coefficient 2 → [BrCl]$^2$.

parts.prompt:
Determine the value of $K_{eq}$
for the decomposition reaction of BrCl at 298 K.

scoring_points.description: For the correct K$_{eq}$ ≈ 0.13 using 42% decomposition: 1 point
scoring_points.official_rubric: P(BrCl) decomposed = (0.42)(1.22 atm) = 0.51 atm; 2x = 0.51 atm → x = 0.26 atm. ICE: P(BrCl)$_{eq}$ = 0.71 atm, P(Br$_2$)$_{eq}$ = P(Cl$_2$)$_{eq}$ = 0.26 atm. $K_{eq} = \frac{(0.26)(0.26)}{(0.71)^2} = 0.13$. 1 point is earned for correct stoichiometric values at equilibrium. 1 point is earned for a consistent value of $K_{eq}$.

sample_responses.commentary: Earns both sub-points: correctly sets up ICE with stoichiometric relationship and calculates K = 0.13.
Does not earn the stoichiometry point: the 42% applies to the initial pressure (1.22 atm), not a concentration of 1.00 M. Must use ICE based on correct initial value.

wrong_examples: Not halving the change in BrCl to get x — if 2x mol of BrCl decomposes, then x mol each of Br$_2$ and Cl$_2$ form.
Using 42% as concentration directly instead of applying to the initial pressure.
common_traps: ICE with pressures: for 2 BrCl → Br$_2$ + Cl$_2$, if P(BrCl) decreases by 2x, then P(Br$_2$) and P(Cl$_2$) each increase by x. 42% of P(BrCl) decomposes → 2x = 0.42 × 1.22 → x = 0.256 atm.

parts.prompt:
Calculate the bond energy of the Br-Cl bond, in kJ/mol, using ΔH ° for the reaction (1.6 kJ/mol
rxn
) and the
information in the following table.
Bond Energy
Bond
(kJ/mol)
Br – Br 193
Cl – Cl 243
Br – Cl ?
parts.scoring_notes: For 2 BrCl(g) → Br$_2$(g) + Cl$_2$(g): ΔH = bonds broken − bonds formed. Bonds broken: 2 Br−Cl (2 × E$_{BrCl}$). Bonds formed: 1 Br−Br (193) + 1 Cl−Cl (243) = 436 kJ/mol. ΔH$°_{rxn}$ = 2E(BrCl) − 436. 1.6 = 2E(BrCl) − 436. 2E(BrCl) = 437.6. E(BrCl) = 218.8 ≈ 219 kJ/mol.

scoring_points.description: For the correct Br−Cl bond energy ≈ 219 kJ/mol: 1 point
scoring_points.official_rubric: $\Delta H° = \Sigma(\text{bond energies})_{broken} - \Sigma(\text{bond energies})_{formed}$; $1.6\text{ kJ/mol} = 2(\text{Br−Cl bond energy}) - (193\text{ kJ/mol} + 243\text{ kJ/mol})$; $(436 + 1.6)\text{ kJ/mol} = 2(\text{Br−Cl bond energy})$; Br−Cl bond energy = 219 kJ/mol. 1 point is earned for a correct calculation of the Br−Cl bond energy.

sample_responses.response: $\Delta H° = \Sigma E_{broken} - \Sigma E_{formed}$. Reactant (2 BrCl): 2 × E(Br−Cl). Products (Br$_2$ + Cl$_2$): E(Br−Br) + E(Cl−Cl) = 193 + 243 = 436 kJ/mol. $1.6 = 2E(\text{Br−Cl}) - 436$. $E(\text{Br−Cl}) = (436 + 1.6)/2 = 218.8 \approx 219\text{ kJ/mol}$.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point: correctly applies the bond energy formula and solves for E(Br−Cl).
Does not earn the point: has the formula backward — $\Delta H° = \Sigma E_{broken} - \Sigma E_{formed}$, not the reverse. Using the wrong sign convention gives an incorrect energy.

required_elements: ΔH = bonds broken − bonds formed = 2E(BrCl) − (193 + 243) = 1.6 kJ/mol
alternatives.correct_example:
ΔH$°_{rxn}$ = Σ(bonds broken) − Σ(bonds formed)
= 2E(Br−Cl) − [E(Br−Br) + E(Cl−Cl)]
= 2E(Br−Cl) − (193 + 243)
1.6 = 2E(Br−Cl) − 436
2E(Br−Cl) = 437.6
E(Br−Cl) ≈ 219 kJ/mol

wrong_examples: Swapping broken and formed: ΔH = formed − broken. The correct formula is ΔH = bonds BROKEN (reactants) − bonds FORMED (products).
Forgetting the factor of 2 for 2 mol Br−Cl broken.
common_traps: Bond enthalpy formula: ΔH = Σ(bonds broken in reactants) − Σ(bonds formed in products). Breaking 2 BrCl → 2 × E(BrCl); forming 1 Br$_2$ + 1 Cl$_2$ → E(Br$_2$) + E(Cl$_2$).
